pick.rationale: Nebraska enters as a top-15 national team ranked 14th in KenPom and 19th in Torvik with elite defensive efficiency (7th nationally at 93.5 adjD) compared to Troy's 142nd/143rd rankings. The efficiency margin gap is staggering at +26.12 for Nebraska versus +1.74 for Troy—a nearly 25-point disparity that reflects superior talent depth and system consistency across every metric. In a classic 4/13 seed matchup where four seeds historically win approximately 70-75% of the time, Nebraska's Big Ten pedigree combined with their 26-6 record and no reported injury concerns makes them the clear favorite to advance past the Sun Belt champion.

Contest Click to expand picks panel Click to open Sharp AI Nebraska vs. Troy: NCAA Tournament Predictions & Preview by BettingPros Staff | @BettingPros | Mar 15, 2026 by BettingPros Staff | @BettingPros | Mar 15, 2026 The 2026 NCAA Tournament is officially here! The March Madness Bracket is set, and first-round NCAA Tournament matchups are in place. It’s time to make your picks and predictions for the first round of the 2026 NCAA Tournament! We’re here to help as we’ll have picks and predictions for each of the first round 2026 NCAA Tournament games. Here are our NCAA Tournament predictions and preview for Nebraska vs. Troy. NCAA Tournament Odds NCAA Tournament Pick’Em Contest NCAA Tournament March Madness Bracket Optimizer NCAA Tournament Betting Trends 2026 NCAA Tournament Predictions & Preview: Nebraska vs. Troy Here are the odds for this opening-round matchup of the 2026 NCAA Tournament. Let’s dive into our preview and predictions for this NCAA Tournament matchup. Nebraska 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview The 2025-26 Cornhuskers are the best team Fred Hoiberg has ever put on the floor in Lincoln, arguably the best in school history. Nebraska started 20-0, entered the AP Top 10 for the first time since 1966, and is 26-6 with a top-10 KenPom defense, allowing opponents to shoot just 40.0% from the field. Pryce Sandfort is as good a junior forward as there is from outside, averaging nearly 18 points per game with three 30-point performances, including a 32-point effort in a win over Illinois. Rienk Mast is the anchor of the frontcourt, capable of finding the open man while also defending the rim. Freshman forward Braden Frager has been solid off the bench, averaging over 11 points a game. Sam Hoiberg, the coach’s son, is the definition of scrappy, averaging over five boards a game despite his 6-0 frame. This whole team could be described as scrappy, with a pack-line defense that has the fifth-best two-point proximity distance against. Opponents get 39 percent of points from beyond the arc, seventh-most in the nation, but the Cornhuskers still hold opponents under 30% from deep. The rebounding vulnerability is real. Michigan, Illinois and Purdue all physically dominated Nebraska on the glass this season, with Purdue securing a 54-37 rebounding edge at Mackey Arena and a 37-29 edge in the Big 10 quarterfinals. The Huskers rank outside the top 300 in offensive boards and free throw rate, leading to just a 70th-rated field goal attempt rate. Their lower shot volume among top-seeded teams is concerning for the tournament. Nebraska is also vulnerable to teams who can sling it from deep, because that's th

You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

- r64-south-1: Florida beat Lehigh

Now I need to analyze Nebraska vs Troy. Let me gather information about both teams using the available tools.

**Nebraska:**
- Torvik rank: 19th

- Record: 26-6

- AdjO: 117.3 (Torvik), 118.6 (KenPom) - ranked 55-62nd nationally
- AdjD: 93.5 (Torvik), 92.5 (KenPom) - ranked 7th in defensive efficiency (Torvik)
- AdjEM: +26.12 (KenPom)

**Troy:**
- Torvik rank: 142nd
- KenPom rank: 143rd
- Record: 22-11
- Conference: Sun Belt (SB)
- AdjO: 110.7 (Torvik), 110.8 (KenPom) - ranked 136-141st nationally
- AdjD: 109.0 (both systems) - ranked 181st defensively
- AdjEM: +1.74 (KenPom)

This is a massive disparity. Nebraska is a top-20 team with elite defensive efficiency (7th nationally in Torvik), while Troy is a mid-tier mid-major team ranked around 140th. The efficiency margin gap is enormous: +26.12 for Nebraska vs +1.74 for Troy - that's nearly a 25-point difference.

Nebraska is clearly the #4 seed and Troy is the #13 seed based on the game label "South 4/13". In Round of 64 matchups, 4-seeds historically win approximately 70-75% of the time against 13-seeds.

The search results don't show any major injury concerns for Nebraska. The articles mention:
- Fred Hoiberg's contract extension and regular season success
- Nebraska has never won an NCAA Tournament game (historical context)
- Some questions about their Big Ten Tournament performance but they still made the tournament

The "fatal flaw" article mentions "lack of size" which could be a concern, but this doesn't seem to be a critical injury issue that would significantly impact their tournament chances.

Given all the data I've gathered:
1. Nebraska is ranked 14th in KenPom and 19th in Torvik - elite level
2. Nebraska has elite defensive efficiency (7th in Torvik at 93.5 adjD)
3. Nebraska's efficiency margin (+26.12) is nearly 25 points better than Troy's (+1.74)
4. Nebraska has a 26-6 record vs Troy's 22-11
5. This is a classic 4/13 seed matchup where 4-seeds win ~70-75% of the time
6. No significant injury concerns reported for either team
7. Nebraska is described as potentially the best team Fred Hoiberg has ever had

Troy is a solid mid-major champion from the Sun Belt, but they're ranked around 140th nationally with average offensive and defensive metrics. They simply don't have the talent or system depth to compete with a top-20 Power 5 team.

I'm confident in picking Nebraska here. The confidence should be high given the massive disparity in metrics and historical precedent.
